_meta.purpose: Reference recommendations per seeded anomaly for the PR F judge. One valid answer shape per anomaly — the judge scores candidate recommendation arrays against the 3-criterion rubric in eval/judges/recommendation-judge.md (plausible action / specific / impact-sized), NOT literal match.
_meta.shape: Each entry is an ARRAY (1-3 items) matching the canonical Recommendation type the agent emits (lib/mcp/types.ts + lib/mcp/validate.ts isRecommendationArray). No `id` field — the agent emits id-less; ids are assigned post-validation in production.
_meta.currency_note: Reais (R$). The Olist DB stores monetary fields in cents; reference impact figures here are written in Reais (whole-Reais rounding) per the PR E finding that the diagnostic agent has a known cents-vs-Reais misquote bug — references are in Reais so the judge has the right unit to score `impact_sized` against.

sp-revenue-drop-w4.title: A/B test free shipping on SP orders under R$100 for 2 weeks
sp-revenue-drop-w4.rationale: SP revenue fell ~30% in week 4 broadly across categories with volume (not price) driving the drop. A free-shipping incentive targeted at the price-sensitive small-cart segment in SP can test whether shipping cost was a friction component and recover conversion before the dip becomes a trend.
steps: Define the SP-state segment of customers placing orders under R$100 in the prior 4 weeks.
Set up an A/B experiment splitting that segment 50/50 — control sees normal shipping, variant sees free shipping.
Run for 2 weeks, then read conversion rate and per-customer revenue between arms.

estimatedImpact.range: +R$120k - R$200k recovered if conversion lifts 5-10% on ~4,200 SP buyers/week
estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 5-10% conversion lift in the variant arm × ~4,200 SP buyers/week × ~R$140 AOV from the diagnosis evidence × 2-week test window.

sp-revenue-drop-w4.successMetric: Conversion rate in the SP-under-R$100 segment: baseline ~6.5%, target +5-10% lift in the variant arm.

sp-revenue-drop-w4.title: Targeted voucher campaign to top-3 SP categories in week following the drop
sp-revenue-drop-w4.rationale: The week-4 SP drop was broad across categories with electronics, fashion, and home_decor all -20% to -35%. A targeted 10% voucher restoring purchase intent in those categories tests demand-side recoverability and gives a quick read on whether the dip was a one-off vs sustained.
steps: Build a customer segment: SP-state buyers who purchased in the last 90 days but not in week 4.
Issue a 10% off voucher valid for electronics/fashion/home_decor purchases for 1 week.
Track redemption rate and incremental revenue against the no-voucher control cohort.

estimatedImpact.range: +R$80k - R$160k recovered if 15-25% redemption on ~3,000 reachable SP recent buyers
estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 15-25% voucher redemption × ~3,000 recent SP buyers × ~R$140 AOV × 90% net (voucher discount cost subtracted).

prerequisites.label: email channel active for SP segment

sp-revenue-drop-w4.successMetric: Week-over-week revenue recovery in SP top-3 categories: baseline -30% in week 4, target return to within -10% of pre-drop baseline.

sp-revenue-drop-w4.title: Investigate fulfillment SLA in SP for week 4 (operational follow-up)
sp-revenue-drop-w4.rationale: The drop is isolated to SP and broad across categories, which is consistent with a local operational cause (carrier delay, warehouse outage). Before launching demand-side fixes, verify whether the upstream cause was supply-side — if a carrier or warehouse caused the dip, demand incentives are the wrong lever.
steps: Define a segment of SP orders placed in week 4 that experienced cancellation or > median delivery time.
Pull the carrier and warehouse for those orders from the fulfillment data; quantify share of SP volume affected.
If > 20% of SP week-4 orders show degraded SLA, route the finding to the ops team and pause demand-side interventions.

estimatedImpact.range: Diagnostic — clarifies whether the R$120k-200k demand-side investment above is targeting the right cause
estimatedImpact.assumption: No direct revenue lift; this is a routing decision that determines whether subsequent demand-side spend is warranted.

prerequisites.label: fulfillment data accessible for the SP week-4 window

sp-revenue-drop-w4.successMetric: Share of SP week-4 orders with degraded SLA: target binary read (>20% or not) within 3 days to gate the demand-side experiments above.

electronics-spike-w2.title: Capture electronics demand pull with a follow-on cross-sell campaign in weeks 3-5
electronics-spike-w2.rationale: Electronics order count spiked 2.5x in week 2 broadly across states with revenue scaling 1:1 with volume — a real demand event, not a vanity-metric artifact. The ~8,800 buyers who entered the funnel in week 2 are a hot cross-sell cohort; a targeted accessories campaign in the 3 weeks after captures incremental revenue at low CAC.
steps: Build a segment of customers who placed an electronics order in week 2 of the dataset.
Design an email campaign featuring electronics-accessory bundles (cases, cables, warranties) at a modest discount.
Send to the segment in weeks 3, 4, 5 with frequency cap of 1 message/week; track attach-rate.

estimatedImpact.range: +R$350k - R$700k incremental over 3 weeks if 5-10% attach rate on ~8,800 buyers at ~R$80 accessory AOV
estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 5-10% accessory attach rate × ~8,800 week-2 electronics buyers × ~R$80 accessory AOV × 3-week campaign window.

electronics-spike-w2.successMetric: Accessory attach rate in the week-2 electronics-buyer cohort over the next 21 days: baseline ~3%, target 5-10%.

electronics-spike-w2.title: Validate driver: identify which electronics SKUs / promotions caused the week-2 spike
electronics-spike-w2.rationale: The diagnosis is consistent with an electronics-specific promotional or seasonal event but doesn't name the driver. Knowing whether it was 3 SKUs or a category-wide promotion changes whether to replicate the lever (re-run the promo) or chase the seasonal window (timing decision).
steps: Define a segment of electronics orders placed in week 2 and identify the top-10 SKUs by order count.
Compare those SKUs' week-2 share of category volume vs their typical share in the 12-week baseline.
If concentration > 50% in top-10, label this as a promo-driven spike; if < 30%, label seasonal pull.

estimatedImpact.range: Diagnostic — drives the decision between repeatable-promo investment (~R$200k+ potential) vs seasonal-timing tweak
estimatedImpact.assumption: No direct revenue; the read gates whether the campaign above is calibrated for a one-off promotional moment or a structural seasonal pattern worth scheduling.

prerequisites.label: SKU-level catalog and order history accessible

electronics-spike-w2.successMetric: Binary read in 2 days: top-10 SKUs share of week-2 electronics volume vs baseline share, classified as promo-driven (>50%) or seasonal (<30%).

electronics-spike-w2.title: A/B test electronics homepage prominence in the weeks following a future spike signal
electronics-spike-w2.rationale: If the spike was driven by demand (vs supply), then surfacing electronics more prominently when the signal returns should compound the effect at near-zero marginal cost. A controlled experiment quantifies the lift and de-risks the always-on alternative.
steps: Set up a 50/50 A/B variant on the homepage hero slot.
Trigger the variant (electronics-promoted hero) when daily electronics order count exceeds 1.5x baseline.
Read conversion uplift and electronics revenue per session in the variant arm over 2 weeks.

estimatedImpact.range: +R$150k - R$400k per future spike window if 3-8% session-conversion lift on the variant
estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 3-8% session-conversion lift × ~100k weekly sessions on the homepage × ~R$200 AOV × 2-week trigger windows occurring 1-2x/quarter.

electronics-spike-w2.successMetric: Session-conversion-rate uplift in the variant arm during trigger windows: target 3-8% relative lift over control.

voucher-dropoff-w10-on.title: Stand up a replacement incentive: 5% off boleto+credit_card for prior voucher users (4-week pilot)
voucher-dropoff-w10-on.rationale: Voucher payment volume collapsed ~95% starting week 10 and order count fell ~8% — part of the lost voucher volume left the platform rather than substituting. The ~12,500 customers who previously used vouchers need a replacement incentive to retain them while the program situation is resolved.
steps: Define a segment of customers who used voucher payment in weeks 1-9 but have not placed an order since week 10.
Issue a 5% discount valid on boleto or credit_card payment, communicated via email.
Pilot for 4 weeks, then read reactivation rate and incremental revenue vs a held-out 10% control cohort.

estimatedImpact.range: +R$700k - R$1.4M recovered over 4 weeks if 10-20% reactivation on ~12,500 dormant voucher users
estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 10-20% reactivation × ~12,500 affected customers × ~R$280 AOV × 95% net (5% discount cost subtracted).

voucher-dropoff-w10-on.successMetric: Reactivation rate of the dormant voucher cohort over 28 days: baseline ~0%, target 10-20% placing an order.

voucher-dropoff-w10-on.title: Resolve the voucher-program root cause (cross-functional ops escalation)
voucher-dropoff-w10-on.rationale: The diagnosis points to a program-level change (cliff onset + sustained 17-week persistence + geographic uniformity). No marketing lever fixes a program/policy gap; the right action is to escalate to the team that owns the voucher program and confirm whether the program is paused, expired, or restricted.
steps: File an escalation with the voucher-program owner including the week-10 cliff chart and the affected-customer count (~12,500).
Confirm program status: paused, expired, vendor-blocked, or fraud-restricted.
If reversible, schedule a re-enablement and target a 4-week ramp to recover the affected customers; if not, formalize the replacement-incentive pilot above as the permanent program.

estimatedImpact.range: Up to R$3M+ recovered annually if the voucher program is restored — bounds the upper end of the dropoff
estimatedImpact.assumption: Bounds estimate: weekly voucher value pre-week-10 × 52 × full restoration. Conservative read assumes resolution within 4-8 weeks.

voucher-dropoff-w10-on.successMetric: Program-status answer in 7 days: paused / expired / vendor-blocked / fraud-restricted, with a re-enable date or a permanent-replacement decision.

voucher-dropoff-w10-on.title: Set up a monitoring scenario for payment-method volume cliffs going forward
voucher-dropoff-w10-on.rationale: A 17-week un-noticed dropoff worth ~R$3M+ annually is itself a finding — there was no alert on payment-method composition. Build the monitoring so the next cliff (any payment type) is caught in week 1, not week 17.
steps: Define a weekly job that compares each payment-method's order-share to its 12-week baseline.
Alert when any payment method's share drops > 50% week-over-week or sustains > 30% drop for 2 consecutive weeks.
Route alerts to the merchandising lead with the affected-customer-count and lost-volume estimate pre-computed.

estimatedImpact.range: Diagnostic — preventing a repeat of this 17-week silent dropoff is worth the full ~R$3M/year exposure
estimatedImpact.assumption: No direct revenue; counterfactual is the next undetected payment-method cliff.

voucher-dropoff-w10-on.successMetric: Time-to-detect on a simulated 50% payment-method dropoff: target < 7 days from onset (current undetected baseline: > 17 weeks).
